title: Jam thumbprint cookies
subtitle: Buttery shortbread rounds with a jam-filled well
excerpt: A classic butter biscuit dough rolled into balls, pressed with a thumb to create a well, and filled with jam before baking. Simple, reliable, and endlessly adaptable to whatever jam is in the cupboard.
sourceNotes: Thumbprint cookies appear across northern European baking traditions under various names, Hallongrotta in Sweden (meaning 'raspberry cave'), Husarenkrapfen in Germany and Austria, and simply 'jam biscuits' in British baking. The method of pressing a well into a ball of shortbread dough and filling it with jam is documented in British household baking books from the late nineteenth century. Florence White includes similar jam-filled butter biscuits in Good Things in England (1932). The recipe here follows the classic British shortbread-style approach.

content.text: The key to a good thumbprint biscuit is a

that is firm enough to roll into balls without cracking but soft enough to take an impression cleanly. Too warm and the wells spread during baking; too cold and the dough cracks when pressed. The dough should be at cool room temperature.

attrs.prepNote: or strawberry, apricot, or any firm-set jam

content.text: Beat the butter and icing sugar together until pale and smooth. Beat in the egg yolk and vanilla. Add the flour and salt and mix until the dough just comes together. Do not overwork it. If it feels too soft to roll, refrigerate for 20 to 30 minutes.

Preheat the oven to 170°C conventional. Line two baking trays with baking paper. Roll the dough into balls of about 18 g each and place them 4 cm apart on the trays. Press a deep well into each ball using your thumb, the handle of a wooden spoon, or a round measuring spoon. The sides should be clean and the dough should not crack. If it does, the dough is too cold; let it warm slightly.

Fill each well with a small amount of jam, just to the level of the dough, do not overfill or the jam will boil over and stick to the tray. Bake for 13 to 15 minutes until the biscuits are pale gold at the edges and firm to the touch. The jam will be bubbling; it sets as it cools. Transfer to a cooling rack. If the wells have puffed back up during baking, press them again gently with a spoon while still warm and top up the jam if needed. Allow to cool completely before storing.
